title: Fitting a wet room floor former and drain
subtitle: Install a pre-formed shower floor former and linear drain in a wet room
excerpt: How to fit a pre-formed polystyrene wet room floor former with an integrated fall toward a linear drain, covering the substrate preparation, bedding the former in tile adhesive, tanking the former surface, and connecting the drain to the waste pipe.
sourceNotes: BS 8102: Protection of below ground structures against water. BS EN 12764: Sanitary installations. NHBC Standards Chapter 7.2 (wet room construction). City and Guilds Level 2 Tiling.

glossaryTerms.definition: A pre-formed panel of rigid expanded polystyrene (EPS), typically 1800 × 900 mm, with a built-in fall (usually 1:50 gradient) toward a central point or an integrated channel drain. The formed slope eliminates the need for a screed to create the fall. Tiles are laid directly over the tanked former surface.

A waterproof barrier applied over a floor or wall substrate to prevent water penetration. In wet room construction, typically a brush-applied or mat-reinforced liquid membrane applied over the floor former and up the walls to at least 150 mm above the floor level. The tiles are laid into the tanking membrane while it is still wet or onto a layer of tile adhesive applied over the cured membrane.

content.text: gives a level-access shower area without the need for a screed to form the fall. The polystyrene panel is lightweight, carries a pre-formed slope, and is tiled directly once

. On a concrete floor this is a half-day job; on a timber-joisted floor the substrate needs reinforcing first with fibre cement board to prevent flex cracking the waterproofing. The drain connection is the most critical step, any leak at the drain will be invisible and devastating.

Wet room floor former with integrated linear drain (sized to the shower area). Linear drain tile insert (to match the floor tile thickness). Liquid waterproof tanking membrane and waterproof tape (for joints and wall-floor junction). Flexible tile adhesive (cement-based, rated for wet rooms). Tile grout (flexible, for wet areas). Access cover or grate for the linear drain.

The subfloor must be clean, level to within 3 mm in 2 m, and completely rigid. On a concrete floor, sweep and vacuum, then fill any depressions with floor levelling compound. On a timber-joisted floor, screw 6 mm fibre cement board across the existing floorboards at 200 mm intervals to eliminate any flex. The fibre cement board must be tight and creak-free before anything is laid over it.
Step 2, position the former and mark the drain location

Set the former dry in position and mark the centre of the drain outlet on the subfloor. Check the level of the former with the spirit level across the high (inlet) edge; the former should sit flat at the high side and slope toward the drain. Cut the subfloor and any necessary notching in the joists for the drain outlet pipe to run clear of obstructions.
Step 3, connect the drain to the waste pipe

Before bedding the former, connect the drain outlet to the waste pipe run. The drain connection is made first because once the former is bedded in adhesive the drain outlet is locked in position. Use appropriate waste fittings with enough fall (1:40 minimum) on the horizontal waste run to prevent the drain backing up. Test the connection is watertight by pouring water through before the former is fixed.
Step 4, bed the former and apply tanking membrane

Apply a bed of flexible tile adhesive to the subfloor using a notched edge to give a full-coverage bed. Press the former firmly into the adhesive and check it is level at the high edge and that the drain end is lower. Allow the adhesive to cure for 24 hours. Once cured, apply two coats of liquid tanking membrane across the entire former surface, up the walls to at least 150 mm height, and lining all corners with the waterproof tape bedded in the first coat. Allow to cure between coats per the manufacturer's instruction.
Step 5, tile the floor and fit the drain cover

Apply flexible tile adhesive over the cured tanking membrane and tile the floor in the normal way, working from the high (wall) edge toward the drain. Cut tiles to fit around the drain channel. Grout with flexible wet-room grout. Fit the drain insert tile into the linear drain channel, cut flush with the floor tiles. Install the drain grate or cover over the drain channel when the grout is fully cured.

attrs.symptom: Water pools on the floor and drains slowly
attrs.cause: The former was not fully seated flush at the high edge, creating a reverse fall in a section of the floor.
attrs.fix: If caught before tanking, re-bed the former with additional adhesive under the low section. If discovered after tiling, investigate whether the pooling area is at a grout joint (fill with flexible sealant) or indicates a former installation issue that requires re-laying.

attrs.symptom: Damp patch appears on the ceiling below within months of use
attrs.cause: Leak at the drain connection or insufficient tanking at the wall-floor junction.
attrs.fix: Isolate the leak by testing the drain outlet separately (pour water into the drain bypassing the floor) and the wall junction (dam the floor and fill with water). The leak source determines which element needs replacing.

attrs.symptom: Tiles crack in the shower area
attrs.cause: Timber subfloor flex was not adequately stiffened before installation.
attrs.fix: Remove affected tiles. Add more screws to the fibre cement board through the subfloor, or add more fibre cement if the existing layer is less than 6 mm. Re-tile.
